title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - IX
content:
On moonlit heath and lonesome bank
    The sheep beside me graze;
    And yon the gallows used to clank
    Fast by the four cross ways.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - I - 1887
content:
From Clee to heaven the beacon burns,
    The shires have seen it plain,
    From north and south the sign returns
    And beacons burn again.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXVI
content:
Along the fields as we came by
    A year ago, my love and I,
    The aspen over stile and stone
    Was talking to itself alone.
    "Oh who

title: The sigh that heaves the grasses
content:
The sigh that heaves the grasses
    Whence thou wilt never rise
    Is of the air that passes
    And knows not if it sighs.

    The diamond

title: The night is freezing fast,
When first my way to fair I took
content:
When first my way to fair I took
    Few pence in purse had I,
    And long I used to stand and look
    At things I could not buy.

    Now t

title: The First Of May
content:
The orchards half the way
    From home to Ludlow fair
    Flowered on the first of May
    In Mays when I was there;
    And seen from stile

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XIII
content:

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XLI
content:
In my own shire, if I was sad
    Homely comforters I had:
    The earth, because my heart was sore,
    Sorrowed for the son she bore;
    An

title: Could man be drunk for ever
content:
Could man be drunk for ever
    With liquor, love, or fights,
    Lief should I rouse at morning
    And lief lie down of nights.

    But men

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XLV
content:
If it chance your eye offend you,
    Pluck it out, lad, and be sound:
    'Twill hurt, but here are salves to friend you,
    And many a balsa

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XLIX
content:
Think no more, lad; laugh, be jolly:
    Why should men make haste to die?
    Empty heads and tongues a-talking
    Make the rough road easy w

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XLIII - The Immortal Part
content:
When I meet the morning beam,
    Or lay me down at night to dream,
    I hear my bones within me say,
    "Another night, another day."

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXIX - The Lent Lily
content:
'Tis spring; come out to ramble
    The hilly brakes around,
    For under thorn and bramble
    About the hollow ground
    The primroses are

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XI
content:
On your midnight pallet lying
    Listen, and undo the door:
    Lads that waste the light in sighing
    In the dark should sigh no more;

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXX
content:
Others, I am not the first,
    Have willed more mischief than they durst:
    If in the breathless night I too
    Shiver now, 'tis nothing ne

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XX
content:
Oh fair enough are sky and plain,
    But I know fairer far:
    Those are as beautiful again
    That in the water are;

    The pools and ri

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LIII - The True Lover
content:
The lad came to the door at night,
    When lovers crown their vows,
    And whistled soft and out of sight
    In shadow of the boughs.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XLII - The Merry Guide
content:
Once in the wind of morning
    I ranged the thymy wold;
    The world-wide air was azure
    And all the brooks ran gold.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XLIV
content:
Shot? so quick, so clean an ending?

    Yours was not an ill for mending,

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XVI
content:
It nods and curtseys and recovers
    When the wind blows above,
    The nettle on the graves of lovers
    That hanged themselves for love.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXXII
content:
From far, from eve and morning
    And yon twelve-winded sky,
    The stuff of life to knit me
    Blew hither: here am I.

    Now- for a bre

title: The rain,

title: Oh stay at home, my lad, and plough
content:
Oh stay at home, my lad, and plough
    The land and not the sea,

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XLVI
content:
Bring, in this timeless grave to throw,

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXI - Bredon Hill[1]
content:
In summertime on Bredon
    The bells they sound so clear;
    Round both the shires they ring them
    In steeples far and near,

title: Soldier from the wars returning,
content:
Soldier from the wars returning,
    Spoiler of the taken town,
    Here is ease that asks not earning;
    Turn you in and sit you down.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXIII
content:

    There's men from the barn and the forge and the mill and the fold,

content:

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXXIV - The New Mistress
content:

    You may be good for something, but you are not good for me.

title: Her strong enchantments failing,
content:

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - L
content:
Clunton and Clunbury,
    Clungunford and Clun,
    Are the quietest places
    Under the sun. 

    In valleys of springs of rivers,

title: As I gird on for fighting
content:
As I gird on for fighting
    My sword upon my thigh,
    I think on old ill fortunes
    Of better men than I.

    Think I, the round world

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXXVI
content:
White in the moon the long road lies,
    The moon stands blank above;
    White in the moon the long road lies
    That leads me from my love.

title: Epitaph On An Army Of Mercenaries

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XL
content:
Into my heart an air that kills

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXI - Bredon Hill [1]
content:
In summertime on Bredon
The bells they sound so clear;
Round both the shires they ring them
In steeples far and near,
A happy noise to hear.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - II
content:
Loveliest of trees, the cherry now
    Is hung with bloom along the bough,
    And stands about the woodland ride
    Wearing white for Eastert

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LXI - Hughley Steeple
content:
The vane on Hughley steeple
    Veers bright, a far-known sign,
    And there lie Hughley people,
    And there lie friends of mine.
    Tall

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XV
content:
Look not in my eyes, for fear

    And there you find your face too clear
    And love it and be lost like

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LXIII
content:
I Hoed and trenched and weeded,

    I brought them home unheeded;
    The hue was not the wear.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXXV
content:
On the idle hill of summer,
    Sleepy with the flow of streams,
    Far I hear the steady drummer
    Drumming like a noise in dreams.

    F

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XIV
content:
There pass the careless people
    That call their souls their own:
    Here by the road I loiter,
    How idle and alone.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LVIII
content:

    Two friends kept step beside me,
    Two honest lads and hale.

title: The fairies break their dances
content:
The fairies break their dances

    And up from India glances

title: Hell�s Gate

content:

content:

title: When I would muse in boyhood
content:

    And nurse resolves and fancies

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XLVII - The Carpenter's Son
content:
"Here the hangman stops his cart:
    Now the best of friends must part.
    Fare you well, for ill fare I:
    Live, lads, and I will die."

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXVIII - The Welsh Marches
content:
High the vanes of Shrewsbury gleam
    Islanded in Severn stream;
    The bridges from the steepled crest
    Cross the water east and west.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LIX - The Isle Of Portland
content:

    From France to England strown;

content:

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LIV
content:
With rue my heart is laden
    For golden friends I had,
    For many a rose-lipt maiden
    And many a lightfoot lad.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXXI

content:

content:

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LII
content:
Far in a western brookland

    The poplars stand and tremble
    By pools I used to know.

title: When summer�s end is nighing
content:
When summer�s end is nighing
    And skies at evening cloud,
    I muse on change and fortune
    And all the feats I vowed

title: The Culprit
content:
The night my father got me
    His mind was not on me;
    He did not plague his fancy
    To muse if I should be
    The son you see.

    T

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXXVII
content:
As through the wild green hills of Wyre

    And far behind, a fading crest,

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - X - MARCH
content:
The sun at noon to higher air,
    Unharnessing the silver Pair
    That late before his chariot swam,
    Rides on the gold wool of the Ram.

title: In valleys green and still
content:
In valleys green and still
    Where lovers wander maying
    They hear from over hill
    A music playing.

    Behind the drum and fife,

title: When the eye of day is shut,
content:

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXVII
content:

    That I was used to drive
    And hear the harness jingle
    When I was man alive?"

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXXIX
content:

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - IV - Reveille
content:
Wake: the silver dusk returning
    Up the beach of darkness brims,
    And the ship of sunrise burning
    Strands upon the eastern rims.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XVIII
content:
Oh, when I was in love with you,
    Then I was clean and brave,
    And miles around the wonder grew
    How well did I behave.

    And now

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XII
content:
When I watch the living meet,
    And the moving pageant file
    Warm and breathing through the street
    Where I lodge a little while,

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LVII
content:
You smile upon your friend to-day,
    To-day his ills are over;
    You hearken to the lover's say,
    And happy is the lover.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LXII
content:

content:

title: Spring Morning
content:
Star and coronal and bell
    April underfoot renews,
    And the hope of man as well
    Flowers among the morning dews.

title: Yonder see the morning blink:
content:
Yonder see the morning blink:

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XLVIII
content:

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXV
content:
This time of year a twelvemonth past,
    When Fred and I would meet,
    We needs must jangle, till at last
    We fought and I was beat.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - III - The Recruit
content:
Leave your home behind, lad,
    And reach your friends your hand,
    And go, and luck go with you
    While Ludlow tower shall stand.

    O

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LI
content:
Loitering with a vacant eye
    Along the Grecian gallery,
    And brooding on my heavy ill,
    I met a statue standing still.
    Still in m

title: The half-moon westers low, my love,
content:
The half-moon westers low, my love,
    And the wind brings up the rain;
    And wide apart lie we, my love,
    And seas between the twain.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LV
content:
Westward on the high-hilled plains
    Where for me the world began,
    Still, I think, in newer veins
    Frets the changeless blood of man.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - VI
content:
When the lad for longing sighs,
    Mute and dull of cheer and pale,
    If at death's own door he lies,
    Maiden, you can heal his ail.

title: In the morning, in the morning,
content:
In the morning, in the morning,
    In the happy field of hay,
    Oh they looked at one another
    By the light of day.

    In the blue and

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXXVIII
content:
The winds out of the west land blow,
    My friends have breathed them there;
    Warm with the blood of lads I know

title: The West
content:
Beyond the moor and the mountain crest

    The sun is down and drinks away

title: Now dreary dawns the eastern light,
content:
Now dreary dawns the eastern light,
    And fall of eve is drear,
    And cold the poor man lies at night,
    And so goes out the year.

title: Astronomy
content:
The Wain upon the northern steep
    Descends and lifts away.
    Oh I will sit me down and weep
    For bones in Africa.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - XXII
content:
The street sounds to the soldiers' tread,
    And out we troop to see:
    A single redcoat turns his head,
    He turns and looks at me.

title: The laws of God, the laws of man,
content:

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - V
content:
Oh see how thick the goldcup flowers
    Are lying in field and lane,
    With dandelions to tell the hours
    That never are told again.

title: Poems From "A Shropshire Lad" - LVI - The Day Of Battle
content:
